title: Evening Trade: Unlocking Revenue After 3pm
subtitle: Your cafe sits empty 14 hours a day. Some of those hours have customers waiting.

toc.label: Extended Hours: Simple Maths, Complex Execution

metrics.label: dangerous revenue concentration in one daypart
trial period before committing to extended hours
minimum bar for a new daypart

sections.heading: The Revenue Concentration Problem
sections.body:
Most cafes make 60–70% of their revenue between 7:30am and 11am. After the morning rush and lunch, the afternoon is quiet, and by 3pm the cafe is half-empty. By 4pm, many close.

This concentration creates fragility. A rainy Monday morning wipes out a third of the day's revenue with no way to recover. A road closure during peak hours is catastrophic. A new competitor who captures part of the morning crowd impacts your entire business.

Diversifying across dayparts — morning, lunch, afternoon, evening — builds resilience. It also improves the economics of your fixed costs: rent, utilities, and equipment are the same whether you trade 8 hours or 12.

The question is not whether you should try to extend trading. It is which extension makes sense for your location, your customer base, and your team's capacity.

Use T170-01 (Evening Viability Assessment Template) to evaluate honestly:
- Is there local demand for evening food, drinks, or events?
- Can your team cover additional hours without burning out?
- Does your lease allow evening trading?
- What is the incremental cost (staff, utilities, stock) vs. projected revenue?
- Are competitors already serving the evening market?

sections.heading: Extended Hours: Simple Maths, Complex Execution
sections.body:
Opening later sounds simple. The complexity is in the detail.

**Staffing.** Your morning team finishes at 2–3pm. An evening shift needs different people — or split shifts that not everyone wants. Factor in the cost of recruiting, training, and managing a separate crew.

**Energy and atmosphere.** A cafe at 8am feels energetic. The same space at 7pm needs to feel different — warmer lighting, different music, a slower pace. Without adjusting the atmosphere, customers feel like they are sitting in a closed cafe that has not turned off the lights yet.

**Menu.** Morning coffee and pastries do not sell at 6pm. You need an evening proposition: wine and cheese, craft beer and sharing boards, cocktails and cake, or just a great quiet place to work with good coffee. The menu shift requires different stock, different prep, and different skills.

**Licensing.** If you want to serve alcohol, you need a premises licence. This takes 4–8 weeks, costs €100–300, and requires compliance with licensing conditions. Check with your local authority before planning an alcohol-based evening offering.

**Break-even.** Calculate the minimum revenue you need per evening to cover the extra staff, utilities, and stock. If one staff member at €12/hr for 4 hours = €48, and stock costs are €20, you need €68 in revenue just to break even. Add margin expectations and you might need €120–150 per evening. Is that realistic?

Do not extend hours across the whole week immediately. Start with Thursday–Saturday evenings for three months. Measure. Decide.

sections.heading: Evening Concepts That Work
sections.body:
The evening cafe is a different product from the morning cafe. It needs its own identity.

**Natural wine and coffee.** An increasingly popular combination. The same specialty mindset applied to wine: small producers, interesting varietals, knowledgeable staff. Pair with a simple cheese and charcuterie board. Low kitchen complexity, high perceived value.

**Supper club / pop-up kitchen.** Monthly or bi-monthly events with a guest chef or themed menu. Creates buzz, drives social media, fills the space, and requires no permanent kitchen commitment. Ticket sales provide guaranteed revenue.

**Co-working evening.** Extended hours for remote workers who need a space after 5pm. Good wifi, power, quiet, and bottomless filter coffee for a fixed fee. Low effort, steady demand in urban areas.

**Live music / acoustic sessions.** A weekly open-mic night or acoustic set brings a crowd that buys drinks and tells friends. Keep it low-volume — you are a cafe, not a venue. A microphone and a stool is enough.

**Board game / book club nights.** Community-building events that require almost no investment. Provide the games and the space, sell the drinks and snacks. Regular attendees become regulars.

**Evening classes.** Coffee brewing workshops, latte art classes, cupping sessions. Charge per head (€15–30), provide the materials and expertise. Your baristas become instructors and the cafe becomes a destination.

The best evening concept for your cafe depends on your location, your customer base, and your team's interests. Test multiple concepts before committing to one.

sections.heading: The Subscription and Membership Model
sections.body:
Subscriptions create the holy grail of cafe economics: predictable recurring revenue.

Use T171-01 (Subscription Model Economic Calculator) to evaluate options:

**Coffee bag subscription.** Monthly delivery of your house blend or rotating single origins. If your beans cost €5/bag to produce and you charge €12/month, that is €7 margin per subscriber. 100 subscribers = €700/month recurring revenue with zero rent overhead.

**Unlimited filter coffee membership.** €25–35/month for unlimited filter coffee. A regular who visits daily drinks ~22 coffees/month. If each filter costs you €0.30, your cost is €6.60. Even at €25/month, the margin is excellent — and the member is in your cafe every day, buying food and pastries.

**Loyalty pre-pay.** A €40 card loaded with €50 credit. Customers pay upfront, you get cash flow, and the 20% bonus costs you less than 20% because some credit always goes unused.

**VIP membership.** €10/month for 10% discount on everything, priority ordering, and invitations to exclusive events (cuppings, new menu previews). The discount costs less than the guaranteed monthly visit frequency.

The key numbers to model:
- **Customer acquisition cost:** How do you get subscribers? (Counter promotion, email, social media)
- **Churn rate:** What percentage cancel each month? (10% churn means you lose half your subscribers in 7 months)
- **Lifetime value:** Average subscription length × monthly revenue per subscriber
- **Marginal cost:** What does each additional subscriber actually cost you in product?

Start with one subscription product. Test for 3 months. Measure churn and satisfaction before scaling.

sections.heading: Pop-Ups and Markets
sections.body:
Before committing to permanent evening trade or new revenue streams, test the market outside your four walls.

Use T208-01 (Pop-Up Opportunity Tracker) to identify and evaluate opportunities:

**Farmers' markets.** Weekly or monthly presence at a local market. Low commitment (one day), high visibility, direct customer interaction. Sell coffee, baked goods, retail bags. The primary value is not the revenue — it is the marketing. Every market customer is a potential cafe visitor.

**Food festivals.** Higher commitment (full weekend, significant stock) but massive exposure. Good for building brand awareness in a new area. Budget for a branded setup (banner, branded cups, menu board).

**Office pop-ups.** Set up a coffee cart in a local office building lobby for morning service. Low rent (often free or token), captive audience, catering leads. Requires portable equipment and a mobile-friendly menu.

**Collaboration pop-ups.** Partner with a local brewery, wine bar, or restaurant for a one-off event. They provide the space and drinks; you provide the coffee and desserts. Shared audience, shared cost, shared risk.

Pop-up economics:
- Kit: portable espresso machine, grinder, water supply, generator/power = €500–2,000 initial investment
- Stock per event: €50–100 for beans, milk, cups
- Revenue per event: €200–800 depending on footfall
- Marketing value: priceless visibility you cannot buy

Track every pop-up: revenue, cost, contacts made, social media reach. The data tells you which events are worth repeating and which are not.

sections.heading: Testing Before Committing
sections.body:
The biggest mistake in revenue diversification is over-committing before you have data.

Every new initiative should follow the same pattern:

**1. Hypothesis.** "We believe that Thursday evening wine-and-cheese events will generate €200+ in revenue per evening and attract 15+ customers."

**2. Minimum viable test.** Run it four times. Four Thursday evenings. Minimal investment: two cases of wine, a cheese board supplier, one extra staff member, social media promotion.

**3. Measure.** Revenue per evening. Customer count. Repeat visitors. Staff feedback. Customer feedback. Did people enjoy it? Did they come back? Did it interfere with morning prep?

**4. Decide.** If four evenings averaged €250 and customers loved it, you have a concept worth developing. If they averaged €80 and felt forced, stop. No shame in testing and learning.

**5. Scale gradually.** Add a second evening. Expand the menu. Invest in better glassware. Each step validated by data from the previous step.

What NOT to do:
- Sign a contract with a wine supplier for 12 months before you have run a single evening
- Hire two evening staff before you know if anyone will come
- Invest in a full kitchen renovation for a supper club concept you have not tested
- Announce a permanent new offering on social media before you have proved it works

Test cheap. Learn fast. Scale what works. Kill what does not. This discipline is what separates strategic growth from expensive experiments.
